Rafael Mendes: reacted with :white_check_mark:

title: #eng-platform: 1768557720
preview:
Dylan Brooks: trying to nail down Console surface area for batching SLO tiers. question: where do we configure tier (fast/standard/cost-efficient) so it matches policy precedence and doesn’t confuse customers?

Nikhil Sharma: +1. Hosted API wants: if you do nothing, you get a sane default. but also want customers to see what they’re actually getting.

Aditya Rao: Optimize POV: Console should show “Recommended tier” + “Effective tier”. recommendation can be a hint, effective is what’s enforced.

Chloe Martin: we currently have Route Policy editor (per endpoint) where you can pick model + params. adding a “Latency/Cost tier” dropdown there seems straightforward. but what about per-project default / org default?

Priya Natarajan: careful: control plane schema is route policy > project default > platform default. Dedicated also adds tenant override. Console needs to represent that stack w/o implying request-level guarantees.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated requirement: override at tenant/cluster level should be visible and explain precedence. support tickets will happen otherwise.

Jordan Lee: current precedence from config proposal:
1) Dedicated tenant override (cluster-level)
2) Route policy explicit tier
3) Route policy “inherit” -> Project default tier
4) Project default “inherit” -> Platform default tier
5) Safety fallback (model/hw constraints)
Console should reflect “effective tier” after evaluation + reason code.

Marissa Cole: for rollout we also have feature flag / staged enable. should show that too? like “tiered defaults not enabled for this route yet”

Ethan Park: tier eval happens in policy engine at request admission. we can expose decision trace fields: effectiveTier, selectionReason, overrideSource. there’s already a reason code enum coming from runtime (see ENG-18426) but we can mirror at policy level.

Alicia Perez: UX thought: separate “Configuration” vs “Runtime behavior”.
- Config: dropdown w/ “inherit” option.
- Runtime behavior card: Effective tier = X, Why = Y, Overrides = Z.
This avoids customers thinking the dropdown is the only thing affecting it.

Kenji Nakamura: agree. in Route Policy UI: Tier field with states:
- Inherit (default)
- Fast
- Standard
- Cost-efficient
If Inherit, show inline text: “Inherited from Project settings” with link.

Felix Schneider: question on “effective profile vs overrides”: do we show actual knob values (max_wait_ms etc) or just tier?

Aditya Rao: for most users, tier + SLO target. for advanced, show “profile details” accordion. but we need to be cautious w/ hardware-specific values.

Nikhil Sharma: also need “what happens if model can’t support cost-efficient” (e.g., quantization constraint) -> then effective tier may be standard. show that.

Jordan Lee: that maps to reason code like `MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK` or `HW_CLASS_CAP`.

Chloe Martin: if we add an accordion, it should show “profile family” rather than all knobs? like:
- Max batch tokens
- Max wait budget
- Streaming prefill cap
But we can keep it read-only.

Priya Natarajan: + ensure we don’t expose internal fleet details that change per region. maybe show “range” or “current profile version id”.

Ethan Park: we can display:
- Effective tier
- Profile ID (e.g., `rt-1.22:llama3.1-70b:h100:fast:v3`)
- Selection reason code
- Override source
That’s stable and lets support/debug link to internal mapping.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated operators will ask “I set fast on route but it’s still standard” — this would answer if tenant override forces standard.

Marissa Cole: rollout angle: if feature flag disables tiered defaults, effective tier might say “legacy/manual”. need a state.

Jordan Lee: we’ll have reason code `TIER_DEFAULTS_DISABLED` or similar. In that case, UI should show “Tiered defaults disabled for this route (rollout)” and point to “Enable tiered defaults” toggle (if we expose it).

Dylan Brooks: do we actually want to expose that toggle in Console? release plan says staged enablement internal -> subset of hosted -> etc. I’d rather keep flags internal at first.

Nikhil Sharma: +1 keep flag internal. but Console still needs to not lie. maybe just show “Status: Not yet available” and tell them to talk to support.

Alicia Perez: ok: show “Availability” pill.
- Available
- Not enabled (contact support)
- Disabled due to incident/rollback
But only visible to admins? or everyone?

Priya Natarajan: show to project admins/operators; hide from basic users. still debating roles.

Kenji Nakamura: on the Config side we should add at least Project-level default tier in Settings > Inference Defaults. that’s the “don’t repeat for each route” path.

Chloe Martin: implementation: Project Settings page exists; adding “Default batching tier” there + route policy can inherit. minimal.

Felix Schneider: where does “recommended tier” show? maybe on model page / optimize page.

Aditya Rao: Optimize page can show rec based on observed traffic (prompt length distribution, streaming). but initial release might just show a static “standard recommended” + link to docs.

Dylan Brooks: focus this thread on Console surface area for tier selection + effective behavior. propose:
1) Route Policy editor: new field “Batching tier” (inherit/fast/standard/cost-efficient).
2) Project Settings: “Default batching tier” (inherit/fast/standard/cost-efficient).
3) Route details page: “Effective batching tier” card w/ profile id + reason + override source.
4) Dedicated only: Tenant override surfaced on tenant page + banner on route if overridden.

Lauren Bishop: banner is important. “Overridden by Dedicated tenant policy” with link to Tenant overrides page.

Ethan Park: also need to show per-route “effective config” with diff highlighting. customers love “what’s in effect” vs “what I set”.

Chloe Martin: we can add a small “Effective configuration” section that lists:
- Tier
- Model
- Region policy
- Any overrides
But we should avoid showing runtime internal scheduler knobs unless “advanced”.

Alicia Perez: I’ll mock a card:
Effective tier: Fast
Source: Route policy override
Why: Explicit route configuration
Profile: rt-1.22 / fast / v3
Notes: Streaming enabled, wait budget tightened

Jordan Lee: note: “why” should be strictly from reason codes. don’t freeform generate. keep consistent with telemetry.

Felix Schneider: + link to “View decision trace” (JSON) for internal/support users. helpful for escalation.

Nikhil Sharma: for Hosted API customers, request-level tier param exists in SDK work (header/param). Console should acknowledge that: “Requests may override tier if client sets X”.

Priya Natarajan: only if policy allows it. for now I think request-level tier is opt-in. Console should show “Client overrides: disabled/enabled”.

Ethan Park: yes, policy has `allowClientTierOverride` boolean. default false. if true, effective tier becomes “variable”. UI should show “Effective tier: varies by request” and show distribution if we have metrics.

Chloe Martin: ok that’s another field in route policy: “Allow client tier override”. behind advanced toggle.

Marissa Cole: careful: that complicates support. but at least show it read-only if set.

Dylan Brooks: action items:
- Chloe/Alicia: propose UI spec for route editor + effective tier card + banners for Dedicated override.
- Jordan/Ethan: confirm fields we can get from policy eval (`effectiveTier`, `overrideSource`, `reasonCode`, `profileId`, `clientOverrideAllowed`).
- Nikhil/Lauren: sanity check copy so we don’t overpromise SLOs.

Alicia Perez: :thumbsup: will post mocks in Figma by EOW.

Lauren Bishop: :+1: and please include the Dedicated precedence examples (tenant override > route > project > default) in UI help text.

Jordan Lee: I’ll paste draft reason codes here for UI mapping:
- `EXPLICIT_ROUTE_TIER`
- `PROJECT_DEFAULT_TIER`
- `PLATFORM_DEFAULT_TIER`
- `DEDICATED_TENANT_OVERRIDE`
- `MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK`
- `OVERLOAD_MODE_ADJUSTMENT`
- `TIER_DEFAULTS_DISABLED`
We should decide which ones are user-facing vs internal.

Nikhil Sharma: user-facing: explicit/inherited/fallback. overload mode maybe too scary? but customers will notice behavior shifts.

Priya Natarajan: overload mode should be phrased gently: “Capacity protection active” and link to docs.

Chloe Martin: noted. we can gate that detail behind “operator details” section.

Dylan Brooks: cool. will follow up in DES-771 and link this thread.
